headlines.geopolitics: Community honors 'unclaimed' Navy veteran with full military funeral
headlines.economy: DOJ creates new fraud division after billions stolen from Minnesota programs
headlines.domestic: Senate advances sweeping housing bill ahead of midterm elections

metadata.daily_do: Check on a veteran you know today — a text, a call, or just showing up

clips.caption: Duke gets emotional about veteran who died alone
Pringle explains why we honor unknown soldiers
Jax discovers the weirdest workplace harassment case ever

script.text: I've been to a lot of funerals. But when hundreds of strangers show up because nobody else will, that's when you know we still got something left in this country.
Good morning, it's Wednesday, March 11th. I'm Leo Barros, and this is The Friction. Let's start with the world.
First up — in Middle Tennessee yesterday, hundreds of people gathered to bury Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman. He had no known family, no one to claim his remains. So the community claimed him instead.
Oh wow.
Full military honors at the Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery. Veterans groups, community members, people who never met him. They said 'unclaimed' doesn't mean forgotten.
Good for them.
On the economy — Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division. The target? Billions stolen from government programs, particularly in Minnesota.
Minnesota again? What is it with that state?
Blanche says they're going after anyone who steals over a thousand dollars. Quote, 'If you steal $1001, that's a felony, and you are going to prison.'
A thousand dollars? That's, like, groceries for a month.
Good. Maybe people will stop treating taxpayer money like Monopoly cash.
And domestically — the Senate is moving toward passing a comprehensive housing bill. This is significant because housing affordability is expected to be the main issue in the midterms.
Finally. Do you know how long I've been waiting for federal action on housing?
script.direction: excited but trying to contain it

script.text: Since you moved to Portland and realized your studio apartment costs more than Duke's mortgage?
Hey, I earned that mortgage. Three tours and fifteen years of payments.
We'll get into all of that. But first, let's talk about what happened in Tennessee. Because I think this story says something about who we are when nobody's watching.
So Lonnie Wayman, Navy veteran, dies with no known family. Gets labeled as 'unclaimed.' But then word gets out, and hundreds of people show up. Veterans, community members, people who never knew him.
You know what gets me? The VA guy said the paperwork was wrong. That 'unclaimed' was a misnomer because look at all these people claiming him.
But why did it get to that point? Like, shouldn't there be systems in place to prevent veterans from dying alone?
That's the question, right? Is this a beautiful story about community, or a failure of our veteran support systems?
It's both. And honestly? I'm okay with that.
How are you okay with that? We have a whole Department of Veterans Affairs. We spend billions—
Bree, hold on. The government can't track down every family member of every veteran who dies. That's not realistic.
But they can try harder than they currently do. I mean, we have databases, social media, genealogy sites—
Wait, are we about to have the government sliding into people's DMs like, 'Hey, your uncle died, sorry for your loss, please claim remains'?
That's actually not far off from what she's suggesting.
I'm being serious here. We have the technology to find people. We just don't prioritize it.
script.direction: frustrated they're not taking it seriously

script.text: But Duke's point about community response — there's something powerful there. These weren't government employees showing up. These were neighbors.
script.direction: steering back to the heart of the story

script.text: Exactly. Look, I've been to military funerals where the whole extended family shows up, and it's beautiful. But this? This was different.
These people didn't have to be there. They had no obligation. They showed up because it was the right thing to do.
And that's beautiful, it really is. But what about the veterans who die alone and don't get that community response? What about the ones nobody hears about?
script.direction: acknowledging his point but pushing back

script.text: That's the systemic question. How many Lonnie Waymans are there?
I don't know. But I know that when people step up like this, it matters. It sends a message.
What's the message? That we'll honor you in death but maybe not so much in life?
Jax, come on.
No, I'm not being cynical. I'm just saying — this guy served his country, and then he died alone. The beautiful part is what happened after.
That's exactly what I'm getting at. Why does it have to be after?
Okay, so what's the solution? More government tracking? Better veteran outreach programs?
Both. And better integration with community organizations. Faith-based groups, VFW posts, veteran service organizations.
Now that I can get behind. The VFW already does a lot of this work. Give them more resources.
So we're talking about a hybrid approach. Government systems plus community networks.
Right. Because the government piece ensures consistency, but the community piece ensures humanity.
And in the meantime, we celebrate the fact that hundreds of people showed up for a stranger because he wore the uniform.
script.direction: bringing it back to the positive

script.text: Okay, that actually gave me chills. In a good way.
Professor, you've been quiet. What's the historical context here?
Well, this takes me directly to Arlington National Cemetery and the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, which was dedicated in 1921.
After the Great War, thousands of American servicemen were buried in unmarked graves or simply never found. Families had no closure, no place to grieve.
Oh, I see where you're going with this.
The Unknown Soldier became a symbol — not just of one man, but of all the unnamed, unclaimed dead. Every mother could visit that tomb and imagine her son.
What happened in Tennessee yesterday was the same impulse. Lonnie Wayman became every veteran who might die alone. The community claimed him because he represented all of them.
That's exactly right. He wasn't just Lonnie Wayman anymore. He was every guy I served with.
The beauty of the Unknown Soldier tradition is that it transforms individual tragedy into collective meaning. Nobody knows his name, but everyone knows what he represents.
Except we did know Lonnie's name.
Precisely. Which makes this even more powerful. He was known, named, and still unclaimed by family. So the community made him theirs.
There's a Roman tradition called 'collegia' — burial societies that ensured no member would be buried without honor, regardless of family status.
So this isn't just American. It's human.
Every civilization has grappled with the question of how to honor the dead who have no one to honor them. The answer is always the same — the community steps in.
So what we saw yesterday wasn't just nice. It was necessary.
Exactly. When we fail to honor our dead properly, we diminish ourselves as a society. Those people in Tennessee understood that instinctively.
And that's why I'm okay with both things being true — the system failing and the community succeeding.
script.direction: circling back to his earlier point

script.text: I get it now. The community response doesn't excuse the system failure, but it does prove that the values are still there.
Doc, has anyone ever told you that you make everything make sense?
My late wife Peggy used to say I had a gift for making simple things complicated and complicated things simple.
She sounds like she was a wise woman, Professor.
She was indeed. She would have loved this story.
Alright, let's shift gears. Jax, what weird stuff happened this week?
Oh man, do I have stories for you. First up — and I cannot believe this is real — a boss in Ireland repeatedly shouted 'potato' at his Irish employee as workplace harassment.
I'm sorry, what?
Dude, this actually went to court. The employee won a harassment case because his boss kept yelling 'potato' at him. Just, like, randomly during meetings.
That's not funny, that's genuinely awful. That's playing on historical stereotypes about—
No, no, you're absolutely right. It's terrible. I'm laughing at the absurdity that someone thought this was okay.
Please tell me the employee won a significant settlement.
He did. And the boss probably learned that workplace harassment isn't a vegetable-based comedy routine.
What is wrong with people?
Speaking of what's wrong with people — Palantir, the AI surveillance company, deployed their advanced weaponry systems to help the government find office chairs.
Office chairs?
Office chairs. Like, the same AI they use to track terrorists is now optimizing federal furniture procurement. Your tax dollars at work, people.
I don't know whether to laugh or cry.
In fairness, government procurement has always been a bit of a mystery. Perhaps artificial intelligence is exactly what it needs.
Professor, they probably spent more on the AI contract than the actual chairs cost.
That sounds about right for government efficiency.
Oh, and Louisiana schools have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments in every classroom. Because apparently that's what education needed — more wall decorations.
I have so many questions about the separation of church and state implications—
Bree, save it for tomorrow's show. We've got one more story.
Right, last one — a Florida drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. Just, like, human storage. Florida Man strikes again.
script.direction: wrapping up with the weirdest story

script.text: How is that not kidnapping?
Oh, it totally is. He got arrested. But imagine being the storage facility manager who discovered that. 'Sir, your monthly payment is late, also there's a person in unit 47.'
This is why I live in Portland. Weird, but not Florida weird.
Alright, that's enough weird for one day. Let's wrap this up.
Today's Daily Do comes straight from that story in Tennessee. Check on a veteran you know.
It doesn't have to be complicated. A text message. A phone call. Showing up with coffee. Just let them know they're not forgotten.
And if you don't know any veterans personally, find your local VFW post. They'll put you to work.
There are also veteran service organizations that need volunteers. Even an hour a month makes a difference.
The point is, we don't have to wait for someone to die alone to show we care. We can show it while they're alive.
As the poet John Donne wrote, 'No man is an island.' In community, we find our truest selves.
And remember, if your boss starts yelling vegetables at you, that's harassment, not humor. Call a lawyer.
script.direction: one last joke to end on

script.text: Thanks for listening to The Friction. I'm Leo Barros.
Duke Patel.
Bree Aldana.
Dr. Haywood Pringle.
And I'm Jax Mooney. We'll see you tomorrow.
